nt_greek: ἰδοὺ ἡ παρθένος ἐν γαστρὶ ἕξει καὶ τέξεται υἱόν
lxx_greek: ἰδοὺ ἡ παρθένος ἐν γαστρὶ λήμψεται καὶ τέξεται υἱόν

key_differences.note: MT has ʿalmāh (young woman of marriageable age); LXX and Matthew use parthenos (virgin). The LXX translation choice is the theological linchpin of Matthew's fulfillment claim.

interpretation_note: Isaiah's original context addresses Ahaz's crisis (8th c. BC); Matthew reads the text as pointing forward to a greater fulfillment. The LXX translation of עַלְמָה as παρθένος was standard Greek, but Matthew's use is explicitly messianic.

quotation_marker: for so it is written by the prophet
nt_greek: καὶ σύ, Βηθλέεμ γῆ Ἰούδα, οὐδαμῶς ἐλαχίστη εἶ ἐν τοῖς ἡγεμόσιν Ἰούδα
lxx_greek: καὶ σύ, Βηθλεέμ, οἶκος τοῦ Εφραθά, ὀλιγοστὸς εἶ τοῦ εἶναι ἐν χιλιάσιν Ἰούδα
mt_hebrew: וְאַתָּה בֵּית-לֶחֶם אֶפְרָתָה צָעִיר לִהְיוֹת בְּאַלְפֵי יְהוּדָה

key_differences.note: MT says Bethlehem is 'least' (tsaʿir); Matthew reverses to 'by no means least,' adding a positive spin that coheres with the messianic birth making Bethlehem great.

interpretation_note: Matthew's wording conflates Mic 5:2 with 2 Sam 5:2 ('shepherd my people Israel'). The reversal of 'least' may reflect interpretive tradition or a different Vorlage.

nt_greek: ἐξ Αἰγύπτου ἐκάλεσα τὸν υἱόν μου
lxx_greek: ἐξ Αἰγύπτου μετεκάλεσα τὰ τέκνα αὐτοῦ

key_differences.word: τὸν υἱόν μου / לִבְנִי / τὰ τέκνα αὐτοῦ
key_differences.note: MT 'my son' (singular) matches Matthew; LXX renders 'his children' (plural). Hosea's original referent is Israel as God's corporate son. Matthew's fulfillment reading sees Jesus as the true Israel recapitulating the Exodus.

interpretation_note: This is a classic typological fulfillment: Israel (God's son) came out of Egypt; Jesus (God's Son) comes out of Egypt. Matthew sees a pattern of divine redemption repeating in Christ.

nt_greek: φωνὴ ἐν Ῥαμὰ ἠκούσθη, κλαυθμὸς καὶ ὀδυρμὸς πολύς· Ῥαχὴλ κλαίουσα τὰ τέκνα αὐτῆς
lxx_greek: φωνὴ ἐν Ῥαμὰ ἠκούσθη θρήνου καὶ κλαυθμοῦ καὶ ὀδυρμοῦ· Ῥαχὴλ ἀποκλαιομένη
mt_hebrew: קוֹל בְּרָמָה נִשְׁמָע נְהִי בְּכִי תַמְרוּרִים רָחֵל מְבַכָּה עַל-בָּנֶיהָ
interpretation_note: Jeremiah's original context is the Babylonian exile; Rachel (ancestress of Joseph/Ephraim) weeps as her descendants go into captivity. Matthew applies it to the Massacre of the Innocents, seeing a pattern of innocent suffering.

quotation_marker: for this is he who was spoken of by Isaiah
nt_greek: φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ· ἑτοιμάσατε τὴν ὁδὸν κυρίου
lxx_greek: φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ· ἑτοιμάσατε τὴν ὁδὸν κυρίου
mt_hebrew: קוֹל קוֹרֵא בַּמִּדְבָּר פַּנּוּ דֶּרֶךְ יְהוָה

key_differences.note: LXX renders YHWH as κύριος (Lord); the NT application to Jesus implicitly identifies Jesus with YHWH's presence — a high-Christology implication embedded in citation.

interpretation_note: Isaiah 40:3 uses the Hebrew parallelism that places 'in the wilderness' with 'prepare the way.' The LXX (and NT) parsing places 'in the wilderness' with 'a voice crying,' matching John's desert ministry.

nt_greek: οὐκ ἐπ᾽ ἄρτῳ μόνῳ ζήσεται ὁ ἄνθρωπος, ἀλλ᾽ ἐπὶ παντὶ ῥήματι ἐκπορευομένῳ διὰ στόματος θεοῦ
lxx_greek: οὐκ ἐπ᾽ ἄρτῳ μόνῳ ζήσεται ὁ ἄνθρωπος ἀλλ᾽ ἐπὶ παντὶ ῥήματι τῷ ἐκπορευομένῳ διὰ στόματος θεοῦ
mt_hebrew: לֹא עַל-הַלֶּחֶם לְבַדּוֹ יִחְיֶה הָאָדָם כִּי עַל-כָּל-מוֹצָא פִי-יְהוָה יִחְיֶה הָאָדָם
interpretation_note: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy's lesson about Israel's wilderness testing directly back in his own wilderness testing — Jesus as the true Israel succeeding where Israel failed.

nt_greek: τοῖς ἀγγέλοις αὐτοῦ ἐντελεῖται περὶ σοῦ καὶ ἐπὶ χειρῶν ἀροῦσίν σε
lxx_greek: ὅτι τοῖς ἀγγέλοις αὐτοῦ ἐντελεῖται περὶ σοῦ τοῦ διαφυλάξαι σε ἐν πάσαις ταῖς ὁδοῖς σου

key_differences.word: omission of 'to guard you in all your ways'
key_differences.note: The devil quotes Ps 91:11-12 but omits the final phrase 'in all your ways' — subtly removing the condition that the promise is for one walking in God's paths, not testing God.

interpretation_note: The Satan's use of Scripture demonstrates that citation without context can distort meaning. Jesus' counter-citation (Deut 6:16) shows proper biblical interpretation in practice.

nt_greek: οὐκ ἐκπειράσεις κύριον τὸν θεόν σου
lxx_greek: οὐκ ἐκπειράσεις κύριον τὸν θεόν σου
interpretation_note: Jesus cites the Massah incident (Exod 17) where Israel tested God's faithfulness. Jesus refuses to demand a sign, trusting without test.

nt_greek: κύριον τὸν θεόν σου προσκυνήσεις καὶ αὐτῷ μόνῳ λατρεύσεις
lxx_greek: κύριον τὸν θεόν σου φοβηθήσῃ καὶ αὐτῷ λατρεύσεις

key_differences.note: Matthew/Luke have 'worship' (proskyneō); LXX has 'fear' (phobēthēsē); MT has 'fear' (tîrāʾ). The NT variant may reflect a liturgical form of the text or deliberate application to the worship temptation.

interpretation_note: The Shema context (Deut 6) binds exclusive devotion to YHWH. Jesus applies it against the temptation to transfer that devotion to Satan.

quotation_marker: this is he of whom it is written
nt_greek: ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἀποστέλλω τὸν ἄγγελόν μου πρὸ προσώπου σου
lxx_greek: ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἀποστέλλω τὸν ἄγγελόν μου καὶ ἐπιβλέψεται ὁδὸν πρὸ προσώπου μου

key_differences.word: πρὸ προσώπου σου / πρὸ προσώπου μου / לְפָנָי
key_differences.note: MT/LXX: 'before my face' (God speaks in first person). NT: 'before your face' — the address shifts to Jesus (you), making John's ministry preparatory for Jesus specifically, implicitly equating Jesus with YHWH.

interpretation_note: This pronoun shift from 'me' (YHWH) to 'you' (Jesus) is a subtle but profound Christological claim embedded in Jesus' own citation.

quotation_marker: that which was spoken through Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled
nt_greek: ἰδοὺ ὁ παῖς μου ὃν ᾑρέτισα, ὁ ἀγαπητός μου εἰς ὃν εὐδόκησεν ἡ ψυχή μου
lxx_greek: Ιακωβ ὁ παῖς μου ἀντιλήμψομαι αὐτοῦ, Ισραηλ ὁ ἐκλεκτός μου
mt_hebrew: הֵן עַבְדִּי אֶתְמָךְ-בּוֹ בְּחִירִי רָצְתָה נַפְשִׁי

key_differences.note: LXX identifies the servant as Jacob/Israel; Matthew's independent translation removes the national identification, applying it directly to Jesus as the individual Servant.

interpretation_note: Matthew's longest OT quotation; he appears to translate directly from the Hebrew rather than follow the LXX. The servant song's original referent (Israel) is reapplied to Jesus as the true Servant of YHWH.

nt_greek: ἀνοίξω ἐν παραβολαῖς τὸ στόμα μου, ἐρεύξομαι κεκρυμμένα ἀπὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου
lxx_greek: ἀνοίξω ἐν παραβολαῖς τὸ στόμα μου, φθέγξομαι προβλήματα ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς
mt_hebrew: אֶפְתְּחָה בְמָשָׁל פִּי אַבִּיעָה חִידוֹת מִנִּי-קֶדֶם

key_differences.word: κεκρυμμένα ἀπὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου / ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς / מִנִּי-קֶדֶם
key_differences.note: Matthew has 'hidden things from the foundation of the world'; LXX and MT have 'from of old/from ancient times.' Matthew's reading intensifies the eschatological dimension of the parables.

interpretation_note: Psalm 78 is Asaph's historical recital; Matthew sees Jesus' parabolic teaching style fulfilling the pattern of teaching through riddling stories.

nt_greek: ἰδοὺ ὁ βασιλεύς σου ἔρχεταί σοι πραΰς καὶ ἐπιβεβηκὼς ἐπὶ ὄνον
lxx_greek: ἰδοὺ ὁ βασιλεύς σου ἔρχεταί σοι δίκαιος καὶ σῴζων αὐτός, πραΰς καὶ ἐπιβεβηκὼς ἐπὶ ὑποζύγιον
mt_hebrew: הִנֵּה מַלְכֵּךְ יָבוֹא לָךְ צַדִּיק וְנוֹשָׁע הוּא עָנִי וְרֹכֵב עַל-חֲמוֹר

key_differences.note: MT has ʿānî (poor/afflicted/humble); LXX and NT have praus (meek/gentle). Both express the paradox of kingly lowliness.

interpretation_note: Zechariah's oracle is consciously enacted by Jesus as a deliberate prophetic sign-act claiming messianic identity. Matthew conflates Zech 9:9 with Isa 62:11.

nt_greek: ὡσαννά· εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου
lxx_greek: ὦ κύριε, σῶσον δή· ὦ κύριε, εὐόδωσον δή. εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου

key_differences.note: Hosanna is a transliteration of hosha-nnā ('save, please!'). By the first century it had become an acclamation of praise rather than a petition, similar to 'hurray for the king!'

interpretation_note: Psalm 118 is the Great Hallel, sung at Passover. The Passover pilgrims applied it directly to Jesus' entry — intentionally or instinctively evoking messianic hope at precisely the Passover season.

nt_greek: ἐκ στόματος νηπίων καὶ θηλαζόντων κατηρτίσω αἶνον
lxx_greek: ἐκ στόματος νηπίων καὶ θηλαζόντων κατηρτίσω αἶνον

key_differences.note: LXX has 'praise' (ainon); MT has 'strength' (ʿōz). Matthew follows the LXX, which gives a meaning fitting children shouting 'Hosanna.'

interpretation_note: Jesus responds to the chief priests' objection by citing the psalm where children's praise establishes God's glory — defending the spontaneous praise as scripturally fitting.

nt_greek: ἀγαπήσεις κύριον τὸν θεόν σου ἐν ὅλῃ τῇ καρδίᾳ σου καὶ ἐν ὅλῃ τῇ ψυχῇ σου καὶ ἐν ὅλῃ τῇ διανοίᾳ σου
lxx_greek: ἀγαπήσεις κύριον τὸν θεόν σου ἐξ ὅλης τῆς διανοίας σου καὶ ἐξ ὅλης τῆς ψυχῆς σου
mt_hebrew: וְאָהַבְתָּ אֵת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ בְּכָל-לְבָבְךָ וּבְכָל-נַפְשְׁךָ וּבְכָל-מְאֹדֶךָ

key_differences.note: MT has 'with all your might' (meʾōd); NT and LXX substitute 'with all your mind' (dianoia). The NT expands to three elements (heart/soul/mind) from MT's two+might. Luke 10:27 adds 'strength' matching MT.

interpretation_note: The Shema (Deut 6:4-5) is the core confession of Jewish faith. Jesus' citation summarizes the whole law, showing Torah's inner logic centers on love. Paul's love summaries (Rom 13:10; Gal 5:14) follow this reading.

quotation_marker: David himself, in the Spirit, says
nt_greek: εἶπεν κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου· κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου
lxx_greek: εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου

key_differences.note: MT: YHWH speaks to my lord (ʾadōnî, 'my lord'). When LXX renders both as kyrios, the text becomes 'the Lord said to my Lord' — enabling Jesus' argument that the Messiah must be David's Lord, not merely his son.

interpretation_note: The most-cited OT text in the NT. Jesus uses the Greek rendering (both as 'Lord') to pose a riddle about Davidic messianism: if David calls him Lord, the Messiah transcends mere Davidic descent. Underpins NT high Christology.

quotation_marker: then was fulfilled what was spoken by Jeremiah the prophet
nt_greek: καὶ ἔλαβον τὰ τριάκοντα ἀργύρια, τὴν τιμὴν τοῦ τετιμημένου
lxx_greek: καὶ ἔστησαν τὸν μισθόν μου τριάκοντα ἀργυροῦς

key_differences.note: Matthew attributes the Zechariah text to Jeremiah — possibly because the combined citation includes Jer 32 (field purchase) and Zech 11:12-13, or reflects a Jeremiah scroll that opened the prophetic collection. Ancient commentators noted the attribution issue.

interpretation_note: The 30 silver pieces and the field purchase combine Zechariah's shepherd rejected for 30 pieces with Jeremiah's field purchase (Jer 32). Matthew sees the two strands as foreshadowing Judas' betrayal price and the potter's field.

quotation_marker: as it is written in Isaiah the prophet
nt_greek: ἰδοὺ ἀποστέλλω τὸν ἄγγελόν μου πρὸ προσώπου σου … φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ
lxx_greek: ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἀποστέλλω τὸν ἄγγελόν μου (Mal 3:1) … φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ (Isa 40:3)
mt_hebrew: הִנְנִי שֹׁלֵחַ מַלְאָכִי (Mal 3:1) … קוֹל קוֹרֵא בַּמִּדְבָּר (Isa 40:3)

key_differences.note: Mark attributes a conflation of Mal 3:1 + Isa 40:3 solely to Isaiah — consistent with rabbinic practice of attributing composite citations to the more prominent prophet, or reflecting a pre-existing testimony collection.

interpretation_note: Mark opens with the most concentrated OT citation in any Gospel, immediately framing John the Baptist and Jesus within Israel's eschatological hope from the prophets.

quotation_marker: the scroll of Isaiah was given to him
nt_greek: πνεῦμα κυρίου ἐπ᾽ ἐμέ, οὗ εἵνεκεν ἔχρισέν με εὐαγγελίσασθαι πτωχοῖς
lxx_greek: πνεῦμα κυρίου ἐπ᾽ ἐμέ, οὗ εἵνεκεν ἔχρισέν με εὐαγγελίσασθαι πτωχοῖς
mt_hebrew: רוּחַ אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה עָלָי יַעַן מָשַׁח יְהוָה אֹתִי לְבַשֵּׂר עֲנָוִים

key_differences.word: ἀπέσταλκέν με ἰάσασθαι τοὺς συντετριμμένους τῇ καρδίᾳ (absent in some MSS)
key_differences.note: Luke inserts 'to heal the brokenhearted' from Isa 61:1 MT; this phrase is absent from some important LXX manuscripts. Luke's text also omits 'the day of vengeance of our God' from Isa 61:2, ending at jubilee-release.

interpretation_note: Jesus' Nazareth sermon is his inaugural public proclamation, reading from Isaiah as a messianic manifesto. The deliberate stop before 'the day of vengeance' signals a fulfillment now of the favorable year, with vengeance deferred.

nt_greek: εὐθύνατε τὴν ὁδὸν κυρίου
lxx_greek: εὐθύνατε τὰς τρίβους τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν

key_differences.note: John has 'Lord' (kyriou); LXX has 'our God' (theou hēmōn); MT has 'our God' (lēlōhênû). John's substitution of 'Lord' for 'God' parallels the Synoptics and makes the application to Jesus more direct.

interpretation_note: John the Baptist applies Isaiah 40:3 to himself — making himself the herald of YHWH's arrival. In the Fourth Gospel this functions within a broader argument about bearing witness to the Word made flesh.

nt_greek: ὁ ζῆλος τοῦ οἴκου σου καταφάγεταί με
lxx_greek: ὁ ζῆλος τοῦ οἴκου σου κατέφαγέν με

key_differences.note: LXX has past tense 'consumed me'; John shifts to future tense 'will consume me' — turning a lament of David into a prediction of Jesus' fate, pointing forward to his death.

interpretation_note: The disciples' post-resurrection remembrance (v.22) reflects Johannine hermeneutics: Scriptures are understood retrospectively. The tense shift is part of the re-reading.

quotation_marker: our fathers ate the manna in the wilderness; as it is written
nt_greek: ἄρτον ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς φαγεῖν
lxx_greek: ἄρτον ἀγγέλων ἔφαγεν ἄνθρωπος (Ps 78:25); τί ἐστιν τοῦτο; (Exod 16:15)
interpretation_note: The Bread of Life discourse begins with the crowd citing manna as a sign qualifying Moses. Jesus reframes: Moses did not give the true bread — God did, and the true bread is the Son of Man himself.

quotation_marker: so that the word spoken by Isaiah might be fulfilled
nt_greek: κύριε, τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν; καὶ ὁ βραχίων κυρίου τίνι ἀπεκαλύφθη;
lxx_greek: κύριε, τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν; καὶ ὁ βραχίων κυρίου τίνι ἀπεκαλύφθη;
mt_hebrew: מִי הֶאֱמִין לִשְׁמֻעָתֵנוּ וּזְרוֹעַ יְהוָה עַל-מִי נִגְלָתָה
interpretation_note: John uses Isaiah 53 to explain Israel's unbelief as paradoxically fulfilling prophecy. The arm of the Lord (God's power in salvation) is revealed precisely where it seems most absent.

nt_greek: τετύφλωκεν αὐτῶν τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς καὶ ἐπώρωσεν αὐτῶν τὴν καρδίαν
lxx_greek: ἐπαχύνθη γὰρ ἡ καρδία τοῦ λαοῦ τούτου, καὶ τοῖς ὠσὶν αὐτῶν βαρέως ἤκουσαν

key_differences.word: τετύφλωκεν (he blinded) / imperative form in MT
key_differences.note: MT is imperative: 'make the heart of this people fat!' (addressed to Isaiah). LXX and John shift to indicative: 'the heart of this people has become fat.' John's version makes God the active agent of hardening — theologically sharpening the text.

interpretation_note: Isaiah 6:9-10 is the most-cited OT passage in the NT for explaining Jewish unbelief (Matt, Mark, Luke-Acts, John, Paul in Romans). John connects it to Isaiah 53 — both in the same vision (Isa 6:1 / John 12:41 says Isaiah 'saw his [Jesus'] glory').

nt_greek: ὁ τρώγων μου τὸν ἄρτον ἐπῆρεν ἐπ᾽ ἐμὲ τὴν πτέρναν αὐτοῦ
lxx_greek: ὁ ἐσθίων ἄρτους μου ἐμεγάλυνεν ἐπ᾽ ἐμὲ πτερνισμόν
mt_hebrew: גַּם-אִישׁ שְׁלוֹמִי אֲשֶׁר-בָּטַחְתִּי בוֹ אוֹכֵל לַחְמִי הִגְדִּיל עָלַי עָקֵב

key_differences.note: John uses trōgōn (to gnaw/munch) — the same word used in John 6:54-58 for eating Jesus' flesh in the Eucharistic discourse. The word choice may link Judas' betrayal to the Eucharistic context of the Last Supper.

interpretation_note: David's trusted friend (Ahithophel?) who ate at his table and betrayed him provides the typological pattern. Judas, who shared the Last Supper, enacts the archetype. John's selection of trōgōn instead of esthiōn is probably deliberate.

quotation_marker: that the Scripture might be fulfilled
nt_greek: διεμερίσαντο τὰ ἱμάτιά μου ἑαυτοῖς καὶ ἐπὶ τὸν ἱματισμόν μου ἔβαλον κλῆρον
lxx_greek: διεμερίσαντο τὰ ἱμάτιά μου ἑαυτοῖς καὶ ἐπὶ τὸν ἱματισμόν μου ἔβαλον κλῆρον
mt_hebrew: יְחַלְּקוּ בְגָדַי לָהֶם וְעַל-לְבוּשִׁי יַפִּילוּ גוֹרָל
interpretation_note: Psalm 22 is the Passion Psalm par excellence — John cites it directly at the cross, and the psalm also underlies the cry of dereliction (Matt 27:46). John's theology of fulfillment is most explicit here: the soldiers unknowingly fulfill Scripture.

quotation_marker: that the Scripture might be fulfilled
nt_greek: ὀστοῦν οὐ συντριβήσεται αὐτοῦ
lxx_greek: φυλάσσει πάντα τὰ ὀστᾶ αὐτῶν, ἓν ἐξ αὐτῶν οὐ συντριβήσεται (Ps 34:20) / καὶ ὀστοῦν οὐ συντρίψετε ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ (Exod 12:46)
mt_hebrew: שֹׁמֵר כָּל-עַצְמוֹתָיו אַחַת מֵהֵנָּה לֹא נִשְׁבָּרָה / וְעֶצֶם לֹא תִשְׁבְּרוּ-בוֹ
interpretation_note: John sees the Passover lamb requirement (no broken bones, Exod 12:46) and Psalm 34's protection of the righteous man combined in Jesus' intact bones. Jesus is both the Passover Lamb and the Righteous Sufferer.

quotation_marker: this is what was uttered through the prophet Joel
nt_greek: ἐν ταῖς ἐσχάταις ἡμέραις, λέγει ὁ θεός, ἐκχεῶ ἀπὸ τοῦ πνεύματός μου ἐπὶ πᾶσαν σάρκα
lxx_greek: καὶ ἔσται μετὰ ταῦτα καὶ ἐκχεῶ ἀπὸ τοῦ πνεύματός μου ἐπὶ πᾶσαν σάρκα

key_differences.word: ἐν ταῖς ἐσχάταις ἡμέραις / μετὰ ταῦτα / אַחֲרֵי-כֵן
key_differences.note: Peter substitutes 'in the last days' for LXX/MT 'after these things' — a deliberate eschatological framing that declares Pentecost as the beginning of the end-time age.

interpretation_note: Peter's Pentecost sermon interprets the Spirit-outpouring as the fulfillment of Joel's eschatological promise. The 'last days' substitution is Peter's (or Luke's) interpretive gloss, not a textual variant.

nt_greek: προορώμην τὸν κύριον ἐνώπιόν μου διὰ παντός … οὐ γὰρ ἐγκαταλείψεις τὴν ψυχήν μου εἰς ᾅδην, οὐδὲ δώσεις τὸν ὅσιόν σου ἰδεῖν διαφθοράν
lxx_greek: προορώμην τὸν κύριον ἐνώπιόν μου διὰ παντός … οὐκ ἐγκαταλείψεις τὴν ψυχήν μου εἰς ᾅδου
mt_hebrew: שִׁוִּיתִי יְהוָה לְנֶגְדִּי תָמִיד … כִּי לֹא-תַעֲזֹב נַפְשִׁי לִשְׁאוֹל לֹא-תִתֵּן חֲסִידְךָ לִרְאוֹת שָׁחַת

key_differences.note: LXX ᾅδης = Sheol (realm of the dead); both translations match. Key interpretive shift: Peter argues that since David died and his tomb is present (v.29), the psalm cannot refer to David himself — it must refer to someone who did not see decay, i.e. the resurrected Christ.

interpretation_note: Peter's Pentecost sermon: the first explicit Christian interpretation of a Davidic psalm as referring to Christ's resurrection. The 'holy one' and 'not see decay' function as proof-texts for the resurrection.

quotation_marker: David did not ascend into the heavens; but he himself says
nt_greek: εἶπεν κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου· κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου
lxx_greek: εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου
interpretation_note: Peter uses the same argument as Jesus in Matt 22:44 — David calls the Messiah 'my Lord,' implying the Messiah's exalted status exceeds Davidic descent. The conclusion (v.36) is that God has made this Jesus 'Lord and Christ.'

nt_greek: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ λίθος ὁ ἐξουθενηθεὶς ὑφ᾽ ὑμῶν τῶν οἰκοδόμων, ὁ γενόμενος εἰς κεφαλὴν γωνίας
lxx_greek: λίθον ὃν ἀπεδοκίμασαν οἱ οἰκοδομοῦντες, οὗτος ἐγενήθη εἰς κεφαλὴν γωνίας
mt_hebrew: אֶבֶן מָאֲסוּ הַבּוֹנִים הָיְתָה לְרֹאשׁ פִּנָּה
interpretation_note: Originally a psalm about Israel's vindication after rejection, applied in the NT to Jesus' rejection by the Jewish leadership and resurrection as vindicating exaltation. Jesus himself used this text (Matt 21:42).

quotation_marker: as also it is written in the second Psalm
nt_greek: υἱός μου εἶ σύ, ἐγὼ σήμερον γεγέννηκά σε
lxx_greek: υἱός μου εἶ σύ, ἐγὼ σήμερον γεγέννηκά σε
interpretation_note: Paul's Pisidian Antioch sermon cites Ps 2:7 as fulfilled in the resurrection: God 'begat' the Son in the sense of raising him from the dead. This correlates 'today I have begotten you' with the resurrection day, not the eternal generation or the baptism.

quotation_marker: as for the fact that he raised him from the dead
nt_greek: δώσω ὑμῖν τὰ ὅσια Δαυὶδ τὰ πιστά
lxx_greek: δώσω ὑμῖν τὰ ὅσια Δαυὶδ τὰ πιστά
mt_hebrew: וְאֶכְרְתָה לָכֶם בְּרִית עוֹלָם חַסְדֵי דָוִד הַנֶּאֱמָנִים

key_differences.note: LXX 'the holy things of David' (ta hosia Dauid); MT 'the sure mercies of David' (ḥasdê dāwid). LXX's 'holy things' (hosia) allows Paul to infer 'not see corruption' — since the holy promises must be fulfilled in someone who doesn't decay.

interpretation_note: Paul uses the LXX rendering to construct a three-text proof for the resurrection (Ps 2:7 + Isa 55:3 + Ps 16:10). The LXX's 'holy things' creates the verbal link to Ps 16:10's 'holy one' who doesn't see decay.

nt_greek: ὁ δὲ δίκαιος ἐκ πίστεως ζήσεται
lxx_greek: ὁ δὲ δίκαιος ἐκ πίστεώς μου ζήσεται

key_differences.word: ἐκ πίστεώς μου (LXX) / ἐκ πίστεως (Paul) / בֶּאֱמוּנָתוֹ (MT)
key_differences.note: MT: 'by his faithfulness' (beʾĕmûnātô, possessive 3ms suffix = his own faithfulness). LXX: 'by my faithfulness' (pisteos mou, 1s = God's faithfulness). Paul omits the pronoun entirely, allowing both readings and enabling his 'from faith to faith' argument about righteousness by faith.

interpretation_note: This is the thesis text of Romans, also the foundation of Galatians 3:11 and Hebrews 10:38. The removal of the possessive pronoun is theologically significant: Paul's argument about faith operates on the ambiguity between 'God's faithfulness' and 'human faith' — both are in view in his theology.

nt_greek: ὅπως ἂν δικαιωθῇς ἐν τοῖς λόγοις σου καὶ νικήσεις ἐν τῷ κρίνεσθαί σε
lxx_greek: ὅπως ἂν δικαιωθῇς ἐν τοῖς λόγοις σου καὶ νικήσεις ἐν τῷ κρίνεσθαί σε

key_differences.note: MT: 'when you judge' (God as active judge); LXX: 'when you are judged' (God as the one being adjudicated). Paul uses the passive LXX reading to argue that God is vindicated even when Israel's unfaithfulness is exposed.

interpretation_note: Paul uses David's confession to support the proposition that human sinfulness actually highlights divine faithfulness. God's righteousness is paradoxically vindicated by the covenant violations that expose Israel's need.

nt_greek: οὐκ ἔστιν δίκαιος οὐδέ εἷς, οὐκ ἔστιν ὁ συνίων
lxx_greek: οὐκ ἔστιν ποιῶν χρηστότητα, οὐκ ἔστιν ἕως ἑνός (Ps 14:1-3)
interpretation_note: Paul's catena (chain quotation) technique: assembling multiple OT texts addressed to Israel and applying them universally to all humanity. This was a standard rabbinic demonstration technique but Paul extends the scope from national to universal condemnation.

nt_greek: ἐπίστευσεν δὲ Ἀβραὰμ τῷ θεῷ, καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην
lxx_greek: καὶ ἐπίστευσεν Αβραμ τῷ θεῷ, καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην

key_differences.note: MT: 'he [God] reckoned it to him'; LXX: passive 'it was reckoned to him' (ἐλογίσθη). Paul's entire argument in Rom 4 and Gal 3 turns on the bookkeeping metaphor: imputation/reckoning of righteousness as credit, not wages.

interpretation_note: Paul's proof that justification by faith predates the Law and circumcision (Gen 15:6 is before Gen 17's circumcision). The LXX passive verb enables Paul's juridical imputation language. James 2:23 cites the same text for 'faith active in works.'

nt_greek: ἕνεκεν σοῦ θανατούμεθα ὅλην τὴν ἡμέραν, ἐλογίσθημεν ὡς πρόβατα σφαγῆς
lxx_greek: ὅτι ἕνεκεν σοῦ θανατούμεθα ὅλην τὴν ἡμέραν, ἐλογίσθημεν ὡς πρόβατα σφαγῆς
mt_hebrew: כִּי-עָלֶיךָ הֹרַגְנוּ כָל-הַיּוֹם נֶחְשַׁבְנוּ כְּצֹאן טִבְחָה
interpretation_note: Paul uses Israel's corporate suffering psalm to interpret Christian suffering in the light of Israel's pattern. The transition from Ps 44 (complaint) to Rom 8:37 ('more than conquerors') mirrors the lament-to-trust movement of the Psalter.

quotation_marker: but through Isaac shall your offspring be named
nt_greek: ἐν Ἰσαὰκ κληθήσεταί σοι σπέρμα
lxx_greek: ἐν Ισαακ κληθήσεταί σοι σπέρμα
interpretation_note: Paul's argument in Rom 9 about election begins here: not all physical descendants of Abraham are the true 'seed' — election has always operated within Israel (Isaac not Ishmael, Jacob not Esau). This is the basis for his defense of God's faithfulness despite Israel's rejection of the gospel.

nt_greek: ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι
lxx_greek: ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι
interpretation_note: Paul uses the Jacob-Esau oracle as proof that election operates by divine choice before birth, not human merit. The present-tense argument: 'that God's purpose of election might continue' (v.11) — election is not national privilege but sovereign grace.

nt_greek: τὸν Ἰακὼβ ἠγάπησα, τὸν δὲ Ἠσαῦ ἐμίσησα
lxx_greek: ἠγάπησα τὸν Ιακωβ, τὸν δὲ Ησαυ ἐμίσησα

key_differences.note: In Malachi the context is national fate (Edom vs. Israel), not individual predestination. 'Hate' in Hebrew idiom often means 'love less' (Gen 29:31 uses same pattern). Paul uses the text as evidence of sovereign election, but the original context is corporate and historical.

interpretation_note: This is the interpretive flashpoint for Calvinist vs. Arminian debates. Paul moves from corporate/national (Malachi's original context) to a principle about divine election. How much the individual/corporate distinction matters for Paul's argument is a major debate.

nt_greek: ἐλεήσω ὃν ἂν ἐλεῶ, καὶ οἰκτιρήσω ὃν ἂν οἰκτίρω
lxx_greek: ἐλεήσω ὃν ἂν ἐλεῶ, καὶ οἰκτιρήσω ὃν ἂν οἰκτείρω
mt_hebrew: וְחַנֹּתִי אֶת-אֲשֶׁר אָחֹן וְרִחַמְתִּי אֶת-אֲשֶׁר אֲרַחֵם
interpretation_note: Exod 33:19 is God's self-declaration after the Golden Calf — mercy is entirely at God's sovereign initiative, not conditioned on Israel's worthiness. Paul uses this as the ground of election: 'so then it depends not on human will or exertion, but on God, who has mercy' (v.16).

quotation_marker: for the Scripture says to Pharaoh
nt_greek: εἰς αὐτὸ τοῦτο ἐξήγειρά σε, ὅπως ἐνδείξωμαι ἐν σοὶ τὴν δύναμίν μου
lxx_greek: ἕνεκεν τούτου διετηρήθης, ἵνα ἐνδείξωμαι ἐν σοὶ τὴν ἰσχύν μου
mt_hebrew: בַּעֲבוּר זֹאת הֶעֱמַדְתִּיךָ בַּעֲבוּר הַרְאֹתְךָ אֶת-כֹּחִי

key_differences.note: MT: 'I have caused you to stand' (heʿĕmadtîkā); LXX: 'you were preserved' (dietērēthēs, passive); Paul: 'I raised you up' (exēgeírá se). Paul's verb is stronger — divine raising up for a purpose, not merely preservation.

interpretation_note: Paul uses Pharaoh as the negative example in his election argument: as Jacob (mercy) and Esau (hardening) illustrate sovereign freedom, so God's mercy in Moses and hardening in Pharaoh demonstrate that the potter has authority over the clay.

quotation_marker: Moses writes about the righteousness that is based on the law
nt_greek: ὁ ποιήσας αὐτὰ ἄνθρωπος ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς
lxx_greek: ἃ ποιήσας ἄνθρωπος ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς
mt_hebrew: אֲשֶׁר יַעֲשֶׂה אֹתָם הָאָדָם וָחַי בָּהֶם
interpretation_note: Paul sets up a contrast between 'righteousness from law' (Lev 18:5 — do and live) and 'righteousness from faith' (Deut 30:12-14). He does not argue Lev 18:5 was impossible — the contrast is about covenant-level operation, not individual sinlessness.

quotation_marker: but the righteousness based on faith says
nt_greek: τίς ἀναβήσεται εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν; … τίς καταβήσεται εἰς τὴν ἄβυσσον; … ἐγγύς σού ἐστιν τὸ ῥῆμα
lxx_greek: τίς ἀναβήσεται ἡμῶν εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν; … τίς διαπεράσει ἡμῶν εἰς τὸ πέραν τῆς θαλάσσης
mt_hebrew: מִי יַעֲלֶה-לָּנוּ הַשָּׁמַיְמָה … מִי יַעֲבָר-לָנוּ אֶל-עֵבֶר הַיָּם

key_differences.word: εἰς τὴν ἄβυσσον / εἰς τὸ πέραν τῆς θαλάσσης / אֶל-עֵבֶר הַיָּם
key_differences.note: MT/LXX: 'who will cross over to the other side of the sea?' Paul substitutes 'who will descend into the abyss?' — allegorizing the sea as the underworld/realm of death, applying the text to Christ's descent and resurrection.

interpretation_note: Paul's allegorical/midrashic reading is one of the most creative OT uses in the NT. Deut 30 was about the accessibility of the Law; Paul appropriates it to say the gospel is equally accessible, no feat of ascent or descent required — Christ has already done both.

nt_greek: πᾶς γὰρ ὃς ἂν ἐπικαλέσηται τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου σωθήσεται
lxx_greek: καὶ ἔσται πᾶς ὃς ἂν ἐπικαλέσηται τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου σωθήσεται
mt_hebrew: וְהָיָה כֹּל אֲשֶׁר-יִקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה יִמָּלֵט

key_differences.note: Joel's YHWH becomes NT's Kyrios. Verse 9 establishes that confessing 'Jesus is Lord' = calling on the name of YHWH. Paul's logic: if calling on the Lord saves, and Jesus is Lord, then calling on Jesus' name is the covenantal call to YHWH.

interpretation_note: The kyrios/YHWH identification is one of the clearest points in Paul where NT Christology elevates Jesus to divine status through OT citation. This same text underlies Acts 2:21 (Peter's Pentecost sermon) with identical implications.

nt_greek: οἱ ὀνειδισμοὶ τῶν ὀνειδιζόντων σε ἐπέπεσαν ἐπ᾽ ἐμέ
lxx_greek: οἱ ὀνειδισμοὶ τῶν ὀνειδιζόντων σε κατέπεσαν ἐπ᾽ ἐμέ
interpretation_note: Paul reads Ps 69 as the voice of Christ (same psalm cited in John 2:17 and Rom 11:9-10). Christ bore the reproaches of those who reproach God — the pattern for Christian behavior toward the weak (Rom 15:1-3).

nt_greek: ἐπικατάρατος πᾶς ὃς οὐκ ἐμμένει πᾶσιν τοῖς γεγραμμένοις ἐν τῷ βιβλίῳ τοῦ νόμου τοῦ ποιῆσαι αὐτά
lxx_greek: ἐπικατάρατος πᾶς ἄνθρωπος ὃς οὐκ ἐμμενεῖ ἐν πᾶσι τοῖς λόγοις τοῦ νόμου τούτου

key_differences.note: Paul's text adds 'in all things' (pasin) to the LXX — MT has 'who does not confirm the words of this law' without 'all.' The addition strengthens the impossibility of full Torah-keeping, supporting Paul's argument that all who rely on the law are under curse.

interpretation_note: Paul's argument in Gal 3:10-13 combines three texts (Deut 27:26, Hab 2:4, Deut 21:23) to argue that the law brings curse, faith brings life, and Christ bore the curse to redeem. Each text choice is precise and deliberate.

nt_greek: ἐπικατάρατος πᾶς ὁ κρεμάμενος ἐπὶ ξύλου
lxx_greek: ὅτι κεκατηραμένος ὑπὸ θεοῦ πᾶς κρεμάμενος ἐπὶ ξύλου

key_differences.word: ὑπὸ θεοῦ absent in Paul / present in LXX
key_differences.note: LXX has 'cursed by God' (hypo theou); Paul omits 'by God' — since Christ bearing the curse for us cannot be 'by God' in the punitive sense Paul's argument requires. Or Paul uses a different LXX manuscript tradition.

interpretation_note: Deut 21:23 originally referred to criminals whose executed bodies were displayed and had to be buried before nightfall. Paul reads crucifixion as the ultimate enactment of this curse, which Christ bore in our place.

quotation_marker: it does not say 'and to offsprings,' referring to many, but 'and to your offspring,' referring to one
nt_greek: καὶ τῷ σπέρματί σου
lxx_greek: καὶ τῷ σπέρματί σου

key_differences.note: Paul argues from the grammatical singular of σπέρμα (seed/offspring) that the promise was made to one seed = Christ, not many seeds = Israel collectively. Critics note σπέρμα is a collective singular in both Hebrew and Greek. Paul's reading is midrashic and Christocentric, not grammatically novel.

interpretation_note: This is the most discussed example of Paul's midrashic interpretation. Whether Paul intends a strict grammatical argument or a typological-Christocentric reading has been debated since Chrysostom. Many scholars see it as a typological claim: the singular promise finds its fullness in the single heir.

nt_greek: εὐφράνθητι, στεῖρα ἡ οὐ τίκτουσα
lxx_greek: εὐφράνθητι, στεῖρα ἡ οὐ τίκτουσα
interpretation_note: Paul's allegory of Hagar and Sarah identifies the two covenants: Sinai (Hagar/slavery) and the new covenant (Sarah/freedom). Isa 54's barren woman who becomes the mother of many is applied to the church as the true Sarah-inheritance.

nt_greek: ἀναβὰς εἰς ὕψος ᾐχμαλώτευσεν αἰχμαλωσίαν, ἔδωκεν δόματα τοῖς ἀνθρώποις
lxx_greek: ἀνέβης εἰς ὕψος, ᾐχμαλώτευσας αἰχμαλωσίαν, ἔλαβες δόματα ἐν ἀνθρώπῳ
mt_hebrew: עָלִיתָ לַמָּרוֹם שָׁבִיתָ שֶּׁבִי לָקַחְתָּ מַתָּנוֹת בָּאָדָם

key_differences.note: MT and LXX: the ascended one 'received' gifts. Paul changes to 'gave gifts to men.' This is either a different textual tradition, or a deliberate interpretive inversion: the king who receives tribute redistributes it as patron — a known ancient practice. Targum Ps 68:18 also has 'gave,' suggesting an Aramaic tradition Paul may follow.

interpretation_note: One of the most striking OT variations in the NT. The Psalm depicts God/the king ascending to Zion with captives. Paul applies it to Christ's ascension and the giving of Spirit-gifts to the church. The verb change from 'received' to 'gave' is the interpretive move that makes it work for his argument.

quotation_marker: for to which of the angels did God ever say
nt_greek: υἱός μου εἶ σύ, ἐγὼ σήμερον γεγέννηκά σε
lxx_greek: υἱός μου εἶ σύ, ἐγὼ σήμερον γεγέννηκά σε
interpretation_note: Hebrews opens its catena of seven OT texts demonstrating Christ's superiority to angels. Ps 2:7 was the coronation formula for Davidic kings, elevated here to eternal Sonship. 'Today' is taken as referring to the eternal 'today' of the Son's exaltation.

nt_greek: ἐγὼ ἔσομαι αὐτῷ εἰς πατέρα, καὶ αὐτὸς ἔσται μοι εἰς υἱόν
lxx_greek: ἐγὼ ἔσομαι αὐτῷ εἰς πατέρα, καὶ αὐτὸς ἔσται μοι εἰς υἱόν
mt_hebrew: אֲנִי אֶהְיֶה-לּוֹ לְאָב וְהוּא יִהְיֶה-לִּי לְבֵן
interpretation_note: Nathan's oracle to David promised that his son (Solomon) would be God's son. Hebrews applies the fuller sense of the Davidic covenant to Christ as the Son in whom all the covenant promises find their 'Yes and Amen.'

nt_greek: τί ἐστιν ἄνθρωπος ὅτι μιμνῄσκῃ αὐτοῦ … ἠλάττωσας αὐτὸν βραχύ τι παρ᾽ ἀγγέλους
lxx_greek: τί ἐστιν ἄνθρωπος ὅτι μιμνῄσκῃ αὐτοῦ … ἠλάττωσας αὐτὸν βραχύ τι παρ᾽ ἀγγέλους

key_differences.note: MT: 'a little lower than God (elohim)'; LXX: 'a little lower than angels (angelous).' The LXX's angelological translation enables Hebrews' whole argument: Jesus was made lower than angels (in the incarnation) and then exalted above them.

interpretation_note: Hebrews reads Psalm 8's 'human being' (ʾĕnôsh) as pointing to the Son of Man. The LXX translation of ʾĕlōhîm as 'angels' is crucial: Christ's incarnation = temporary subjection 'lower than angels'; resurrection = exaltation over all things.

quotation_marker: when Christ came into the world, he said
nt_greek: θυσίαν καὶ προσφορὰν οὐκ ἠθέλησας, σῶμα δὲ κατηρτίσω μοι
lxx_greek: θυσίαν καὶ προσφορὰν οὐκ ἠθέλησας, ὠτία δὲ κατηρτίσω μοι
mt_hebrew: זֶבַח וּמִנְחָה לֹא-חָפַצְתָּ אָזְנַיִם כָּרִיתָ לִּי

key_differences.word: σῶμα (body) / ὠτία (ears) / אָזְנַיִם כָּרִיתָ (ears you dug)
key_differences.note: MT: 'ears you have dug for me' (a possible idiom for attentive obedience or making someone able to hear). LXX: 'ears you have prepared for me.' Hebrews has 'a body you have prepared for me' — a significant change that makes the Incarnation and Christ's bodily sacrifice the direct referent.

interpretation_note: This is the most striking textual difference in the NT's OT citations. The LXX's 'ears' vs Hebrews' 'body' may reflect a different manuscript tradition, a scribal confusion of ὠτία/σῶμα, or a deliberate interpretive rendering. Hebrews' entire argument about Christ's body as the final sacrifice depends on 'body' rather than 'ears.'

nt_greek: ἔτι γὰρ μικρὸν ὅσον ὅσον, ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἥξει καὶ οὐ χρονίσει
lxx_greek: ἐκδέχομαι ὅτι ἥξει καὶ οὐ μὴ χρονίσῃ (Hab 2:3 LXX)
mt_hebrew: כִּי עוֹד חָזוֹן לַמּוֹעֵד וְיָפֵחַ לַקֵּץ וְלֹא יְכַזֵּב

key_differences.word: ὁ ἐρχόμενος (the coming one) / Hab 2:3 impersonal
key_differences.note: Habakkuk 2:3 speaks of the vision itself coming ('it will certainly come'). LXX personalizes slightly. Hebrews adds the definite article: 'the Coming One' (ho erchomenos) — a messianic title, transforming an impersonal statement about a vision into a statement about Christ's return.

interpretation_note: Hebrews combines Isa 26:20 (a little while) with Hab 2:3-4 (the coming one, the righteous shall live by faith) to exhort patient endurance. The messianic personalization of Hab 2:3 transforms it from general eschatology to Christological hope.

nt_greek: ἰδοὺ τίθημι ἐν Σιὼν λίθον ἐκλεκτὸν ἀκρογωνιαῖον ἔντιμον, καὶ ὁ πιστεύων ἐπ᾽ αὐτῷ οὐ μὴ καταισχυνθῇ
lxx_greek: ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἐμβαλῶ εἰς τὰ θεμέλια Σιων λίθον πολυτελῆ ἐκλεκτὸν ἀκρογωνιαῖον ἔντιμον
mt_hebrew: הִנְנִי יִסַּד בְּצִיּוֹן אֶבֶן אֶבֶן בֹּחַן פִּנַּת יִקְרַת מוּסָד מוּסָד

key_differences.note: MT: 'the one who believes will not be in haste' (lo yaḥîsh — will not flee in panic). LXX and NT: 'will not be put to shame' (ou kataischynthēsetai). The LXX shift changes the promise from tactical coolness in crisis to eschatological vindication for faith.

interpretation_note: Peter assembles a Zion stone-complex (Isa 28:16 + Ps 118:22 + Isa 8:14) to describe Christ as the foundational stone who is both the ground of faith and the stone of stumbling. The same complex appears in Rom 9:33.

nt_greek: βασίλειον ἱεράτευμα, ἔθνος ἅγιον, λαὸς εἰς περιποίησιν
lxx_greek: βασίλειον ἱεράτευμα καὶ ἔθνος ἅγιον (Exod 19:6)

key_differences.note: MT: 'a kingdom of priests' (mamleket kōhănîm — priests who constitute a kingdom). LXX: 'a royal priesthood' (basileion hierateuma — priesthood that is royal). The LXX renders as an adjective+noun rather than two nouns, changing the nuance from political to sacerdotal.

interpretation_note: Peter applies the Sinai covenant formula to the church, drawn from both Israel's identity (Exod 19) and the Servant Israel's vocation (Isa 43). The transfer of Israel's covenant identity to the church is a major theological move underlying Peter's ecclesiology.

quotation_marker: behold he is coming with the clouds
nt_greek: ἰδοὺ ἔρχεται μετὰ τῶν νεφελῶν … ὄψεται αὐτὸν πᾶς ὀφθαλμὸς καὶ οἵτινες αὐτὸν ἐξεκέντησαν
lxx_greek: ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἤρχετο (Dan 7:13) … καὶ ἐπιβλέψονται πρὸς μέ ἀνθθ᾽ ὧν κατωρχήσαντο (Zech 12:10 LXX)
mt_hebrew: וַאֲרוּ עִם-עֲנָנֵי שְׁמַיָּא (Dan 7:13) … וְהִבִּיטוּ אֵלַי אֵת אֲשֶׁר-דָּקָרוּ (Zech 12:10)

key_differences.word: ἐξεκέντησαν (pierced) / δωρχήσαντο (LXX: 'mocked' — possible textual corruption) / דָּקָרוּ (MT: pierced)
key_differences.note: MT Zech 12:10: 'they will look on me, the one they have pierced.' LXX is corrupt/mistranslated here. Revelation follows the MT reading rather than the LXX, as does John 19:37.

interpretation_note: Revelation's opening verse is a conflation of Daniel's Son of Man vision and Zechariah's pierced-one mourning. John regularly conflates without citation, creating a new composite text. Here the MT is followed over LXX, showing John's independence from LXX in some cases.
